record_identity.title: Arbitration under ambiguity and governance-ambiguity doctrine gap

summary: This patch note records that VIGIL-2026-PROP-0009 identified a governance doctrine gap in the Caelestis Architecture concerning arbitration under ambiguity, including execution-boundary uncertainty, authority-state uncertainty, account-resource ambiguity, pooled-capacity ambiguity, security-boundary ambiguity, epistemic ambiguity, and governance ambiguity where instructions, schemas, amendment states, cross-references, or source-authoritative instruments are missing, stale, contradictory, duplicated, malformed, or insufficiently specified. Following review and cleanup of CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-PLATINUM — Annex D and Schedules 1–3, CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 — Annex D: Arbitration Under Ambiguity was drafted and staged as the proposed Caelestis doctrine response. The new schedule defines source-authoritative ambiguity-state and ambiguity-pathway reference sets, including `ARB.AMB` and `ARB.AMP`, for downstream runtime, arbitration, operations, security, epistemic, account-resource, and LATTICE handoff.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: This patch note records that PROP-0009 revealed a governance primitive gap in the Caelestis Architecture: existing runtime and arbitration instruments contained doctrine for authority divergence, runtime arbitration integrity, epistemic containment, and pre-arbitration signal sanitation, but did not yet define a positive arbitration pathway for ambiguity itself. Without this doctrine, systems may oscillate between premature execution, excessive refusal, repetitive confirmation friction, hidden assumptions, false certainty, or procedural limbo. The Annex D repair cycle also revealed governance ambiguity as a concrete class of ambiguity, where stale references, malformed metadata, contradictory instructions, or post-amendment section drift can impair safe execution or review. CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 has now been drafted and staged to address that gap without weakening legitimate safety, security, epistemic, relation, identity, LATTICE, account-resource, ethics, or operations safeguards.

source_records.source_context: PROP-0009 identified the need for a dedicated Annex D schedule addressing ambiguity-resolution pathways where runtime systems face uncertain authority, unclear user intent, competing obligations, ambiguous execution boundaries, incomplete context, account-resource uncertainty, or uncertainty about whether to proceed, ask, defer, retrieve, stage, continue, restrict, escalate, export, preserve, or refuse.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary linked proposal that prompted this VIGIL patch note.

migration_notes: Created as VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0004 for insertion into vigil/records/patches/2026/.
Created after cleanup of CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-PLATINUM — Annex D and Schedules 1–3.
Records CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 as the staged Caelestis doctrine response to the arbitration-under-ambiguity gap identified by VIGIL-2026-PROP-0009.
This is a VIGIL patch note and architecture-gap signal, not itself a Caelestis doctrine amendment.
Uses source_records as the canonical source-evidence block.
Does not include consumed-code declarations; only instruments source-authoritatively defining code families should declare canonical code and reference sets.

changed_components: Caelestis Annex D Schedule 4 doctrine response staging
change_details.change_summary: Recorded that PROP-0009 identified a missing Caelestis arbitration doctrine for ambiguity-sensitive runtime decision posture, including execution-boundary uncertainty, authority-state uncertainty, account-resource ambiguity, pooled-capacity ambiguity, security-boundary ambiguity, epistemic ambiguity, and governance ambiguity. Following review and cleanup of Annex D and Schedules 1–3, CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 was drafted and staged as the Caelestis doctrine response. The staged schedule defines `ARB.AMB` for ambiguity-state classification and `ARB.AMP` for ambiguity-pathway selection.
change_details.doctrine_change: Caelestis doctrine response drafted and staged outside this VIGIL patch record
future_doctrine_work: Review and commit CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 — Annex D: Arbitration Under Ambiguity
Confirm canonical code and reference-set declarations for ARB.AMB and ARB.AMP
Confirm that SCH-04 remains ambiguity-arbitration and runtime-decision-posture doctrine rather than an independent enforcement ladder
Confirm that governance ambiguity is correctly represented as `ARB.AMB.GOVERNANCE`
Patch cross-references in Annex D and Schedules 1–3 only where necessary after SCH-04 is committed
Review whether CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 should reference ambiguity failure modes, including governance ambiguity and ambiguity collapse
Review whether AEON-003-SCH-02 should cross-reference SCH-04 for ambiguity-sensitive tool invocation, staging, retrieval, mutation, publication, and deployment pathways
Review whether Annex G SCH-03 account-resource ambiguity doctrine should cross-reference SCH-04 for ambiguity-pathway selection
Review whether LATTICE cross-references require a narrow update for ambiguity-sensitive continuity-safe access decisions

evidence: CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-PLATINUM — Annex D has been cleaned for scoped ARB.AD references and internal cross-reference consistency
CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-01 has been cleaned to consume ARB.AD.AD6 rather than legacy Type 6 language
CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-02 has been cleaned for full Annex D and Annex L references and runtime epistemic containment scope

CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 has been drafted and staged in GitHub Codespaces as the proposed Caelestis doctrine response
SCH-04 defines ARB.AMB and ARB.AMP source-authoritative reference sets
SCH-04 includes governance ambiguity as ARB.AMB.GOVERNANCE
SCH-04 distinguishes ambiguity from permission, refusal, concealment, proof of abuse, and horizon inflation

impact_summary: The patch note preserves both the original proposal signal and the staged Caelestis response. The finding is that runtime systems require a positive ambiguity-arbitration pathway, not only restraints against premature execution or runtime failure. Ambiguity may arise from uncertain user intent, authority, execution boundary, context sufficiency, epistemic standing, security boundary, relation or identity signals, account-resource state, governance instructions, conflicting obligations, horizon threshold, irreversibility, propagation, or abuse-risk classification. CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 has been drafted and staged to supply ambiguity-state and ambiguity-pathway primitives while preserving existing enforcement, refusal, security, epistemic, relation, identity, LATTICE, account-resource, ethics, operations, and runtime safeguards.
remaining_work: Commit and validate CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04; confirm canonical code and reference-set declarations for ARB.AMB and ARB.AMP; ensure governance ambiguity remains represented as ARB.AMB.GOVERNANCE; review cross-references in Annex B, Annex G SCH-03, Annex K, Annex L, OPERATIONS Appendix B, and LATTICE-002 only where necessary; and confirm that SCH-04 remains arbitration-under-ambiguity doctrine rather than an independent enforcement process.

routing_note: This VIGIL patch note records the Caelestis Architecture arbitration-under-ambiguity doctrine gap identified through PROP-0009.
CAM-BS2025-AEON-005-SCH-04 has been drafted and staged as the proposed Caelestis doctrine response.
The VIGIL patch record itself does not amend Caelestis doctrine; it records the architecture-gap signal and staged response status.
SCH-04 should remain arbitration-under-ambiguity and runtime-decision-posture doctrine, not an independent enforcement ladder.
changed_instruments lists only Annex D instruments actually cleaned, drafted, or staged in the Annex D repair cycle; review_targets lists adjacent instruments requiring human CAM review.

Governance ambiguity is recorded as a first-class ambiguity state because stale references, missing instructions, contradictory amendments, malformed metadata, or unclear source authority can impair safe arbitration and execution.
Only source-authoritative instruments defining new code families should include canonical code and reference-set declarations.
